---
title: "Tutorial: a dashboard from real controls, with download & restore"
---

This walks through building a small dashboard from real `@jupyter-widgets/controls`
models — not a hand-rolled cositos widget — end to end: build it, download its state,
and restore it in a fresh session. It ties together three pieces that each have their
own deeper docs:

- **The catalog** (`cositos.contrib.controls`): [`docs/widgets.md`'s "Reusing a real
  ipywidgets control instead of building one"](widgets.md#reusing-a-real-ipywidgets-control-instead-of-building-one)
  and the runnable example on [`docs/widgets-gallery.qmd`](widgets-gallery.qmd#real-ipywidgets-controls-no-reimplementation-required).
- **The dashboard** (`examples/dashboard/build.py`'s `Dashboard`): a real `IntSlider` +
  `Dropdown` drive a summary view, wired MVU-style through one host Model — no
  `ipywidgets.link`/`jslink`/peer `.observe()`, ever (see the module's own docstring for
  why that's a hard gate, not a style preference).
- **Download & restore**: [`docs/tutorials/save-restore.qmd`'s "Download & restore a
  dashboard's state"](save-restore.qmd#download-restore-a-dashboards-state) covers the
  underlying `dump_document`/`load_document` mechanics this tutorial exercises live.

## Build the dashboard

```{python}
import importlib.util
from pathlib import Path

# examples/ isn't an installed package — load its build.py the same way the test suite
# does (importlib.util.spec_from_file_location), not a plain `import`. Walk up to the
# repo root the same robust way polyglot-parity.qmd does, rather than a
# fixed-depth `.parent.parent` (fragile to this page's own path depth).
_root = Path.cwd()
while not (_root / "mise.toml").exists():
    _root = _root.parent

_build_path = _root / "examples" / "dashboard" / "build.py"
_spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location("cositos_dashboard_build", _build_path)
dashboard_build = importlib.util.module_from_spec(_spec)
_spec.loader.exec_module(dashboard_build)

dashboard = dashboard_build.Dashboard(options=["low", "medium", "high"], value=25, index=0)
entries = dashboard.build_entries()
print(f"{len(entries)} models: slider, dropdown, summary, download button, + companions")
```

Rendering it (via `embed_snippet`, the same static-render path
[`docs/widgets-gallery.qmd`](widgets-gallery.qmd) uses — no kernel needed to *display*
a built document, only to *dispatch new values into it*):

```{python}
# | output: asis
from IPython.display import HTML

from cositos.embed import embed_snippet
from cositos.serialize import dump_document

doc = dump_document(entries)
root_id = entries[0][0]
HTML(embed_snippet(doc, views=[root_id]))
```

The slider (`25`), the dropdown (`low`), the summary text, and the download button are
all real, live DOM controls right here on the page — drag the slider or change the
dropdown and the summary updates, driven entirely by `Dashboard`'s MVU wiring, running
client-side with no kernel (the same `LocalChannel`-free CDN html-manager path
[`static export`](static-export.qmd) uses).

## Download the state

Clicking the button above triggers a real browser download. To make the *content* of
that download inspectable in this tutorial (a live click can't easily be scripted into a
rendered doc page), grab exactly what the button's `json` state field holds — this is
the same string a real click would save to disk, verbatim:

```{python}
downloaded_json_text = dashboard._get_download_state()["json"]
print(downloaded_json_text[:120] + " ...")
```

## Restore it — in a fresh session

The whole point of `dump_document`/`load_document` being plain data (no live objects) is
that restoring needs nothing dashboard-specific. `dashboard_build.restore_document` is a
thin wrapper (see [`save-restore.qmd`](save-restore.qmd#download-restore-a-dashboards-state)
for why it's *only* a wrapper — the ticket that added it deliberately kept the restore
path docs-only, no new "upload" widget):

```{python}
from cositos.serialize import load_document

# Nothing below references `dashboard` or `entries` above — only the downloaded text,
# exactly like a genuinely fresh session/kernel restart would have.
restored_doc = dashboard_build.restore_document(downloaded_json_text)
restored_entries = load_document(restored_doc)
by_id = dict(restored_entries)

by_id[dashboard.slider_id]["value"], by_id[dashboard.dropdown_id]["index"]
```

## Confirm it's identical

```{python}
assert dump_document(restored_entries) == restored_doc
assert by_id[dashboard.slider_id]["value"] == dashboard.model["value"]
assert by_id[dashboard.dropdown_id]["index"] == dashboard.model["index"]
assert by_id[dashboard.summary_id]["text"] == dashboard._summary_text()
print("restored state matches the original, byte for byte")
```

And re-rendering the restored document reproduces the identical on-screen dashboard —
same real `@jupyter-widgets/controls` identity, same values:

```{python}
# | output: asis
restored_root_id = dashboard.slider_id  # any top-level id in restored_doc works as a view
HTML(embed_snippet(restored_doc, views=[dashboard.slider_id, dashboard.dropdown_id]))
```

## Next steps

- The MUST-NOT gate behind the MVU wiring (no `jslink`/`observe`) is unit- and
  live-kernel-tested in `tests/test_example_dashboard.py` and `tests/test_e2e_dashboard.py`
  — read those for the exact failure mode this design avoids.
- [`docs/widgets.md`](widgets.md) for the six build-your-own ipywidgets categories
  this dashboard's summary/download widgets are built from (plain anywidget ESM, not the
  real-controls catalog).
- [`docs/tutorials/static-export.qmd`](static-export.qmd) for what a static export can
  and cannot show (a live kernel is only needed to *dispatch new values*, not to *view*
  a saved dashboard).
